7	/** Trait that adds a `Temporal` layer inside another layer.
8	  *
9	  * This temporal layer can used to guard statements which are unsupported,
10	  * expensive, or otherwise needed to be excluded from normal design
11	  * verification code in certain tools or environments.  E.g., this is intended
12	  * to work around lack of support for certain SystemVerilog Assertions in
13	  * simulators.
14	  *
15	  * @note While this is used to provide temporal sub-layers in Chisel's default
16	  * layers, it is entirely reasonable for users to mix-in this trait into their
17	  * own user-defined layers to provide similar, recognizable functionality.
18	  */
19	trait HasTemporalInlineLayer { this: Layer =>
20	
21	  /** The [chisel3.layer.Layer]] where complicated assertions that may not be
22	    * supported by all tools are placed.
23	      */
24	  object Temporal
25	      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)(
26	        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
27	        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
28	      )
29	}
30	
31	/** The root [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] for all shared verification collateral. */
32	object Verification
33	    extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification"))))(
34	      _parent = implicitly[Layer],
35	      _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
36	    ) {
37	
38	  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all assertions will be placed. */
39	  object Assert
40	      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "assert"))))(
41	        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
42	        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
43	      )
44	      with HasTemporalInlineLayer
45	
46	  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all assumptions will be placed. */
47	  object Assume
48	      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "assume"))))(
49	        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
50	        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
51	      )
52	      with HasTemporalInlineLayer
53	
54	  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all covers will be placed. */
55	  object Cover
56	      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "cover"))))(
57	        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
58	        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
59	      )
60	      with HasTemporalInlineLayer
61	
62	  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all printfs will be placed.  This layer
63	    * _does not_ have a child temporal layer as it is not expected to be
64	    * useful.
65	    */
66	  object Debug
67	      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "debug"))))(

package chisel3.layers

import chisel3.experimental.UnlocatableSourceInfo
import chisel3.layer.{CustomOutputDir, Layer, LayerConfig}
import java.nio.file.Paths

/** Trait that adds a `Temporal` layer inside another layer.
  *
  * This temporal layer can used to guard statements which are unsupported,
  * expensive, or otherwise needed to be excluded from normal design
  * verification code in certain tools or environments.  E.g., this is intended
  * to work around lack of support for certain SystemVerilog Assertions in
  * simulators.
  *
  * @note While this is used to provide temporal sub-layers in Chisel's default
  * layers, it is entirely reasonable for users to mix-in this trait into their
  * own user-defined layers to provide similar, recognizable functionality.
  */
trait HasTemporalInlineLayer { this: Layer =>

  /** The [chisel3.layer.Layer]] where complicated assertions that may not be
    * supported by all tools are placed.
      */
  object Temporal
      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)(
        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
      )
}

/** The root [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] for all shared verification collateral. */
object Verification
    extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification"))))(
      _parent = implicitly[Layer],
      _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
    ) {

  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all assertions will be placed. */
  object Assert
      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "assert"))))(
        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
      )
      with HasTemporalInlineLayer

  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all assumptions will be placed. */
  object Assume
      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "assume"))))(
        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
      )
      with HasTemporalInlineLayer

  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all covers will be placed. */
  object Cover
      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "cover"))))(
        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
      )
      with HasTemporalInlineLayer

  /** The [[chisel3.layer.Layer]] where all printfs will be placed.  This layer
    * _does not_ have a child temporal layer as it is not expected to be
    * useful.
    */
  object Debug
      extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract(CustomOutputDir(Paths.get("verification", "debug"))))(
        _parent = implicitly[Layer],
        _sourceInfo = UnlocatableSourceInfo
      )
}

1	// SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
2	
3	package chisel3
4	
5	import chisel3._
6	import chisel3.internal._
7	import chisel3.internal.binding.OpBinding
8	import chisel3.internal.Builder.pushCommand
9	import chisel3.internal.firrtl.ir._
10	import chisel3.Data.ProbeInfo
11	import chisel3.experimental.{requireIsHardware, SourceInfo}
12	import chisel3.experimental.dataview.reifyIdentityView
13	import chisel3.reflect.DataMirror.{checkTypeEquivalence, collectAllMembers, hasProbeTypeModifier}
14	
15	package object probe extends ProbeObjIntf {
16	
17	  private[chisel3] def setProbeModifier[T <: Data](data: T, probeInfo: Option[ProbeInfo]): Unit = {
18	    probeInfo.foreach { _ =>
19	      collectAllMembers(data).foreach { member =>
20	        member.probeInfo = probeInfo
21	        // also set sample_element's probe information in Vecs
22	        member match {
23	          case v: Vec[_] => v.sample_element.probeInfo = probeInfo
24	          case _ => // do nothing
25	        }
26	      }
27	    }
28	  }
29	
30	  /** Initialize a probe with a provided probe value.
31	    *
32	    * @param sink probe to initialize
33	    * @param probeExpr value to initialize the sink to
34	    */
35	  def define[T <: Data](sink: T, probeExpr: T)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): Unit = {
36	    val (realSink, writable) = reifyIdentityView(sink).getOrElse {
37	      Builder.error(s"Define only supports identity views for the sink, $sink has multiple targets.")
38	      return // This error is recoverable and this function returns Unit, just continue elaboration.
39	    }
40	    writable.reportIfReadOnlyUnit(())
41	    val typeCheckResult = realSink.findFirstTypeMismatch(
42	      probeExpr,
43	      strictTypes = true,
44	      strictWidths = true,
45	      strictProbeInfo = false /* we will check more more detailed probe info below */
46	    )
47	    typeCheckResult.foreach { msg =>
48	      Builder.error(s"Cannot define a probe on a non-equivalent type.\n$msg")
49	    }
50	    requireHasProbeTypeModifier(realSink, "Expected sink to be a probe.")
51	    requireNotChildOfProbe(realSink, "Expected sink to be the root of a probe.")
52	    requireHasProbeTypeModifier(probeExpr, "Expected source to be a probe expression.")
53	    requireCompatibleDestinationProbeColor(
54	      realSink,
55	      s"""Cannot define '$realSink' from colors ${(Builder.layerStack.headOption)
56	          .map(a => s"'${a.fullName}'")
57	          .mkString("{", ", ", "}")} since at least one of these is NOT enabled when '$realSink' is enabled"""
58	    )
59	    if (realSink.probeInfo.get.writable) {
60	      requireHasWritableProbeTypeModifier(
61	        probeExpr,
62	        "Cannot use a non-writable probe expression to define a writable probe."
63	      )
64	    }
65	    pushCommand(ProbeDefine(sourceInfo, realSink.lref, probeExpr.ref))
66	  }
67	
68	  private[probe] def _readImpl[T <: Data](source: T)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): T = {
69	    requireIsHardware(source)
70	    requireHasProbeTypeModifier(source)
71	    // construct clone to bind to ProbeRead
72	    val clone = source.cloneTypeFull
73	    clone.bind(OpBinding(Builder.forcedUserModule, Builder.currentBlock))
74	    clone.setRef(ProbeRead(source.ref))
75	    // return a non-probe type Data that can be used in Data connects
76	    clearProbeInfo(clone)
77	    clone
78	  }
79	
80	  /** Recursively clear ProbeInfo */
81	  private def clearProbeInfo[T <: Data](data: T): Unit = {
82	    data match {
83	      case a: Aggregate => {
84	        a.probeInfo = None
85	        a.elementsIterator.foreach(x => clearProbeInfo(x))
86	      }
87	      case leaf => { leaf.probeInfo = None }
88	    }
89	  }
90	
91	  /** Pad [[Data]] to the width of a probe if it supports padding */
92	  private def padDataToProbeWidth[T <: Data](data: T, probe: Data)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): T = {
93	    // check probe width is known
94	    requireHasProbeTypeModifier(probe, s"Expected $probe to be a probe.")
95	    if (!probe.isWidthKnown) Builder.error("Probe width unknown.")
96	    val probeWidth = probe.widthOption.getOrElse(0)
97	
98	    // check data width is known
99	    data.widthOption match {
100	      case None => Builder.error("Data width unknown.")
101	      case Some(w) =>
102	        if (probe.widthOption.exists(w > _)) Builder.error(s"Data width $w is larger than $probeWidth.")
103	    }
104	
105	    data match {
106	      case d: Bits => d.pad(probeWidth).asInstanceOf[T]
107	      case d => d
108	    }
109	  }
110	
111	  /** Override existing driver of a writable probe on initialization.
112	    *
113	    * @param probe writable Probe to force
114	    * @param value to force onto the probe
115	    */
116	  def forceInitial(probe: Data, value: Data)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): Unit = {
117	    requireHasWritableProbeTypeModifier(probe, "Cannot forceInitial a non-writable Probe.")
118	    pushCommand(ProbeForceInitial(sourceInfo, probe.ref, padDataToProbeWidth(value, probe).ref))
119	  }
120	
121	  /** Release initial driver on a probe.
122	    *
123	    * @param probe writable Probe to release
124	    */
125	  def releaseInitial(probe: Data)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): Unit = {
126	    requireHasWritableProbeTypeModifier(probe, "Cannot releaseInitial a non-writable Probe.")
127	    pushCommand(ProbeReleaseInitial(sourceInfo, probe.ref))
128	  }
129	
130	  /** Override existing driver of a writable probe. If called within the scope
131	    * of a [[when]] block, the force will only occur on cycles that the when
132	    * condition is true.
133	    *
134	    * Fires only when reset has been asserted and then deasserted through the
135	    * [[Disable]] API.
136	    *
137	    * @param probe writable Probe to force
138	    * @param value to force onto the probe
139	    */
140	  def force(probe: Data, value: Data)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): Unit = {
141	    requireHasWritableProbeTypeModifier(probe, "Cannot force a non-writable Probe.")
142	    val clock = Builder.forcedClock
143	    val cond = Module.disableOption.map(!_.value).getOrElse(true.B)
144	    pushCommand(ProbeForce(sourceInfo, clock.ref, cond.ref, probe.ref, padDataToProbeWidth(value, probe).ref))
145	  }
146	
147	  /** Release driver on a probe. If called within the scope of a [[when]]
148	    * block, the release will only occur on cycles that the when condition
149	    * is true.
150	    *
151	    * Fires only when reset has been asserted and then deasserted through the
152	    * [[Disable]] API.
153	    *
154	    * @param probe writable Probe to release
155	    */
156	  def release(probe: Data)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): Unit = {
157	    requireHasWritableProbeTypeModifier(probe, "Cannot release a non-writable Probe.")
158	    val clock = Builder.forcedClock
159	    val cond = Module.disableOption.map(!_.value).getOrElse(true.B)
160	    pushCommand(ProbeRelease(sourceInfo, clock.ref, cond.ref, probe.ref))
161	  }
162	
163	}

3	package chisel3.probe
4	
5	import chisel3._
6	import chisel3.Data.ProbeInfo
7	import chisel3.experimental.{requireIsChiselType, SourceInfo}
8	import chisel3.internal.{containsProbe, requireNoProbeTypeModifier, Builder}
9	
10	/** Utilities for creating and working with Chisel types that have a probe or
11	  * writable probe modifier.
12	  */
13	private[chisel3] trait ProbeBase {
14	
15	  protected def apply[T <: Data](
16	    source:   => T,
17	    writable: Boolean,
18	    _color:   Option[layer.Layer]
19	  )(
20	    implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo
21	  ): T = {
22	    val prevId = Builder.idGen.value
23	    // call Output() to coerce passivity
24	    val data = Output(source) // should only evaluate source once
25	    requireNoProbeTypeModifier(data, "Cannot probe a probe.")
26	    if (containsProbe(data)) {
27	      Builder.error("Cannot create a probe of an aggregate containing a probe.")
28	    }
29	    if (writable && data.isConst) {
30	      Builder.error("Cannot create a writable probe of a const type.")
31	    }
32	    // TODO error if trying to probe a non-passive type
33	    // https://github.com/chipsalliance/chisel/issues/3609
34	
35	    val ret: T = if (!data.mustClone(prevId)) data else data.cloneType.asInstanceOf[T]
36	    // Remap the color if the user is using the ChiselStage --remap-layer option.
37	    val color = _color.map(c =>
38	      Builder.inContext match {
39	        case false => c
40	        case true  => Builder.layerMap.getOrElse(c, c)
41	      }
42	    )
43	    // Record the layer in the builder if we are in a builder context.
44	    if (Builder.inContext && color.isDefined) {
45	      layer.addLayer(color.get)
46	    }
47	    setProbeModifier(ret, Some(ProbeInfo(writable, color)))
48	    ret
49	  }
50	
51	  protected def apply[T <: Data](
52	    source:   => T,
53	    writable: Boolean
54	  )(
55	    implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo
56	  ): T = {
57	    apply(source, writable, None)
58	  }
59	}

13	    */
14	  def apply[T <: Data](source: => T): T = macro chisel3.internal.sourceinfo.ProbeTransform.sourceApply[T]
15	
16	  /** Mark a Chisel type as with a probe modifier and a layer color.
17	    */
18	  def apply[T <: Data](source: => T, color: layer.Layer): T =
19	    macro chisel3.internal.sourceinfo.ProbeTransform.sourceApplyWithColor[T]
20	
21	  /** @group SourceInfoTransformMacro */
22	  def do_apply[T <: Data](source: => T)(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): T =
23	    super.apply(source, false, None)
24	
25	  /** @group SourceInfoTransformMacro */
26	  def do_apply[T <: Data](source: => T, color: Option[layer.Layer])(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): T =
27	    super.apply(source, false, color)
28	}
29	
30	object RWProbe extends ProbeBase with SourceInfoDoc {
31	
32	  /** Mark a Chisel type with a writable probe modifier.
33	    */
34	  def apply[T <: Data](source: => T): T = macro chisel3.internal.sourceinfo.ProbeTransform.sourceApply[T]
35	
36	  /** Mark a Chisel type with a writable probe modifier and a layer color.
37	    */
38	  def apply[T <: Data](source: => T, color: layer.Layer): T =
39	    macro chisel3.internal.sourceinfo.ProbeTransform.sourceApplyWithColor[T]
40

43	    super.apply(source, true, None)
44	
45	  /** @group SourceInfoTransformMacro */
46	  def do_apply[T <: Data](source: => T, color: Option[layer.Layer])(implicit sourceInfo: SourceInfo): T =
47	    super.apply(source, true, color)
48	}

1	---
2	layout: docs
3	title:  "Layers"
4	section: "chisel3"
5	---
6	
7	# Layers
8	
9	Layers describe functionality of a Chisel generator that a user would like to
10	_optionally_ include at Verilog elaboration time.  Pragmatically, they are a
11	feature to access SystemVerilog's `bind` construct and `` `ifdef `` preprocessor
12	macros.  The optional functionality of layers, by construction, is not allowed
13	to affect logic outside the layer.
14	
15	Layers are typically used to describe design verification code or
16	debugging logic that a user would like to be able to later disable (for
17	performance, verbosity, or cleanliness reasons) or use internally, but exclude
18	from delivery to a customer.
19	
20	## Overview
21	
22	A layer consists of two pieces:
23	
24	1. A layer _declaration_, and
25	1. One or more _layer blocks_ inside Chisel modules.
26	
27	The declaration indicates that optional functionality can exist.  The layer
28	block contains the optional functionality.
29	
30	There are two kinds of layers.  The layer kind determines the _convention_,
31	i.e., how the layer blocks of a layer are represented in Verilog and the
32	mechanism to enable a layer.  Available layer kinds are:
33	
34	1. "Extract" Layers: layers whose blocks are lowered to modules that are
35	   instantiated using `bind` and can be enabled by including a file during
36	   Verilog elaboration, and
37	2. "Inline" Layers: layers whose blocks will be guarded with `` `ifdef `` macros
38	   and can be enabled by setting a Verilog preprocessor define.
39	
40	Extract layers may also specify a directory into which their collateral are
41	written.
42	
43	:::info
44	
45	For more information about these SystemVerilog concepts, the IEEE 1800-2023
46	standard discusses `bind` in Section 23.11 and `` `ifdef `` in Section 23.6.
47	
48	:::
49	
50	To declare a layer, create a singleton `object` in scala that extends the
51	abstract class `chisel3.layer.Layer`, passing into the layer constructor either
52	an object of class `chisel3.layer.LayerConfig.Extract` for an extract layer, or
53	the object `chisel3.layer.LayerConfig.Inline` for an inline layer.
54	
55	Below, an extract layer and an inline layer are declared:
56	
57	```scala mdoc:silent
58	import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig}
59	
60	object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
61	
62	object B extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
63	```
64	
65	Layers may be nested.  Nesting a child layer under a parent layer means that the
66	child layer may access constructs in the parent layer.  Put differently, the
67	child layer will only be enabled if the parent layer is already enabled.  To
68	declare a nested layer, extend the `chisel3.layer.Layer` abstract class inside
69	another declaration.
70	
71	The following example defines an extract layer with two nested layers inside it:
72	
73	```scala mdoc:silent
74	object C extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract()) {
75	  object D extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
76	  object E extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline) {
77	    object F extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
78	  }
79	}
80	```
81	
82	:::info
83	
84	SystemVerilog prohibits a `bind` instantiation under another `bind`
85	instantiation.  However, Chisel allows nesting of extract layers.  This is
86	resolved by the FIRRTL compiler to restructure nested extract layers to be
87	sibling modules that communicate via ports.
88	
89	:::
90	
91	:::warning
92	
93	Extract layers may not be nested under inline layers.  However, inline layers
94	may be nested under extract layers.
95	
96	Any module which contains layer blocks or transitively contains layer blocks in
97	its submodules may not be instantiated under a layer block.
98	
99	:::
100	
101	A _layer block_, associated with a layer, adds optional functionality to a
102	module that is enabled if that layer is enabled.  To define a layer block, use
103	the `chisel3.layer.block` inside a Chisel module and pass the layer that it
104	should be associated with.
105	
106	Inside the layer block, any Chisel or Scala value visible in lexical scope may
107	be used.  Layer blocks may not return values.  Any values created inside a layer
108	block are not accessible outside the layer block, unless using layer-colored
109	probes.
110	
111	The following example defines layer blocks inside module `Foo`.  Each layer
112	block contains a wire that captures a value from its visible lexical scope.
113	(For nested layer blocks, this scope includes their parent layer blocks.):
114	
115	```scala mdoc:silent
116	import chisel3._
117	import chisel3.layer.block
118	
119	class Foo extends RawModule {
120	  val port = IO(Input(Bool()))
121	
122	  block(A) {
123	    val a = WireInit(port)
124	  }
125	
126	  block(B) {
127	    val b = WireInit(port)
128	  }
129	
130	  block(C) {
131	    val c = WireInit(port)
132	    block(C.D) {
133	      val d = WireInit(port | c)
134	    }
135	    block(C.E) {
136	      val e = WireInit(port ^ c)
137	      block (C.E.F) {
138	        val f = WireInit(port & e)
139	      }
140	    }
141	  }
142	}
143	```
144	
145	The layer block API will automatically create parent layer blocks for you if
146	possible.  In the following example, it is legal to directly create a layer
147	block of `C.D` directly in a module:
148	
149	```scala mdoc:silent
150	class Bar extends RawModule {
151	  block (C.D) {}
152	}
153	```
154	
155	Formally, it is legal to create a layer block associated with a layer as long as
156	the current scope is an _ancestor_ of the request layer.
157	
158	:::info
159	
160	The requirement is an _ancestor_ relationship, not a _proper ancestor_
161	relationship.  This means that it is legal to nest a layer block under a layer
162	block of the same layer like:
163	
164	```scala mdoc:silent
165	class Baz extends RawModule {
166	  block(A) {
167	    block(A) {}
168	  }
169	}
170	```
171	
172	:::
173	
174	## Verilog ABI
175	
176	Layers are compiled to SystemVerilog using the FIRRTL ABI.  This ABI defines
177	what happens to layer blocks in a Chisel design and how a layer can be enabled
178	after a design is compiled to SystemVerilog.
179	
180	:::info
181	
182	For the exact definition of the FIRRTL ABI for layers, see the [FIRRTL ABI
183	Specification](https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl-spec/releases/latest/download/abi.pdf).
184	
185	:::
186	
187	### Extract Layers
188	
189	Extract layers have their layer blocks removed from the design.  To enable a
190	layer, a file with a specific name should be included in the design.  This file
191	begins with `layers-` and then includes the circuit name and all layer names
192	delimited with dashes (`-`).
193	
194	For example, for module `Foo` declared above, this will produce three files, one
195	for each extract layer:
196	
197	```
198	layers-Foo-A.sv
199	layers-Foo-C.sv
200	layers-Foo-C-D.sv
201	```
202	
203	To enable any of these layers at compilation time, the appropriate file should
204	be included in the build.  Any combination of files may be included.  Including
205	only a child layer's file will automatically include its parent layer's file.
206	
207	### Inline Layers
208	
209	Inline layers have their layer blocks guarded with conditional compilation
210	directives.  To enable an inline layer, set a preprocessor define when compiling
211	your design.  The preprocessor define begins with `layer$` and then includes the
212	all layer names delimited with dollar signs (`$`).  Parent extract layer names
213	appear in the macro.
214	
215	For example, for module `Foo` declared above, this will be sensitive to three
216	macros, one for each inline layer:
217	
218	```
219	layer$B
220	layer$C$E
221	layer$C$E$F
222	```
223	
224	## User-defined Layers
225	
226	A user is free to define as many layers as they want.  All layers shown
227	previously are user-defined, e.g., `A` and `C.E` are user-defined layers.
228	User-defined layers are only emitted into FIRRTL if they have layer block users.
229	To change this behavior and unconditionally emit a user-defined layer, use the
230	`chisel3.layer.addLayer` API.
231	
232	:::tip
233	
234	Before creating new user-defined layers, consider using the built-in layers
235	defined below.  Additionally, if working in a larger project, the project may
236	have it's own user-defined layers that you are expected to use.  This is because
237	the ABIs affect the build system.  Please consult with a technical lead of the
238	project to see if this is the case.
239	
240	:::
241	
242	## Built-in Layers
243	
244	Chisel provides several built-in layers.  These are shown below with their full
245	Scala paths.  All layers except the temporal layers are extract layers.
246	Temporal layers are inline:
247	
248	```
249	chisel3.layers.Verification                          (Extract)
250	├── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assert               (Extract)
251	│   └── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assert.Temporal  (Inline)
252	├── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assume               (Extract)
253	│   └── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assume.Temporal  (Inline)
254	├── chisel3.layers.Verification.Cover                (Extract)
255	│   └── chisel3.layers.Verification.Cover.Temporal   (Inline)
256	└── chisel3.layers.Verification.Debug                (Extract)
257	```
258	
259	These built-in layers are dual purpose.  First, these layers match the common
260	use case of sequestering verification code.  The `Verification` layer is for
261	common verification collateral.  The `Assert`, `Assume`, and `Cover` layers are
262	for, respectively, assertions, assumptions, and cover statements.  The
263	`Temporal` layers can be used to sequester _temporal_ properties as these are
264	not fully supported by simulation tools.  Second, the Chisel standard library
265	uses them for a number of its APIs.  _Unless otherwise wrapped in a different
266	layer block, the following operations are automatically placed in layers_:
267	
268	* Assertions are placed in the `Verification.Assert` layer
269	* Assumptions are placed in the `Verification.Assume` layer
270	* Covers are placed in the `Verification.Cover` layer
271	* Prints are placed in the `Verification.Debug` layer
272	
273	At this time, no operations are automatically placed in `Temporal` layers.  The
274	`Temporal` layer is up to the user to use as needed.
275	
276	:::tip
277	
278	User-defined layers may want to define their own `Temporal` layer.  To ease
279	this, Chisel provides the `chisel3.layers.HasTemporalInlineLayer` which, when
280	mixed-in to a user-defined layer, will add a child inline temporal layer.
281	
282	:::
283	
284	:::warning
285	
286	Users of the ChiselSim library should manually disable `Temporal` layers when
287	using `Temporal` layers to describe properties that their chosen simulator
288	cannot suppot.  If using ChiselSim's `Cli.Simulator` trait to support multiple
289	backends, then the Verilator backend will automatically disable temporal layers.
290	
291	:::
292	
293	For predictability of output, these layers will always be show up in the FIRRTL
294	that Chisel emits.  To change this behavior, use `firtool` command line options
295	to _specialize_ these layers (remove their optionality by making them always
296	enabled or disabled).  Use `-enable-layers` to enable a layer, `-disable-layers`
297	to disable a layer, or `-default-layer-specialization` to set a default
298	specialization.
299	
300	:::tip
301	
302	Users may extend built-in layers with user-defined layers using an advanced API.
303	To do this, the layer parent must be specified as an implicit value.
304	
305	The following example nests the layer `Debug` to the `Verification` layer:
306	
307	```scala mdoc:silent
308	object UserDefined {
309	  // Define an implicit val `root` of type `Layer` to cause layers which can see
310	  // this to use `root` as their parent layer.  This allows us to nest the
311	  // user-defined `Debug` layer under the built-in `Verification` layer.
312	  implicit val root: Layer = chisel3.layers.Verification
313	  object Debug extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
314	}
315	```
316	
317	:::
318	
319	## Layer-coloring
320	
321	While layers are not allowed to influence the design or their parent layers, it
322	is often useful and necessary to allow layer blocks to send information out of
323	their containing modules to be read by layer blocks of the same layer or
324	children layers. Hardware which has this optional property is said to be
325	_layer-colored_.  Both probes and wires can be layer-colored.
326	
327	### Layer-colored Probes and Wires
328	
329	A layer-colored probe is a probe that exists if a user enables its corresponding
330	layer during Verilog elaboration.  Layer-colored probes are used to describe
331	optional verification, debugging, or logging interfaces.
332	
333	Layer-colored wires are used as temporary storage of defined probe values.  They
334	are used for communication between layer blocks of the same layer in the same
335	module or as temporary storage when forwarding a probe to a port.
336	
337	A layer-colored probe or wire may be the target of a `define` if the `define` is
338	enabled when the color of the probe or wire is enabled.  A layer-colored probe
339	or wire may be `read` from if the color of the probe or wire is enabled when the
340	`read` is enabled.  Put differently, you may write to your layer or a child
341	layer and you may read from your layer or a parent layer.
342	
343	:::info
344	
345	For more information, see the layer coloring section of the [FIRRTL
346	Specification](https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl-spec/releases/latest/download/spec.pdf).
347	
348	:::
349	
350	The example below shows two layer-colored probe ports and one layer-colored
351	probe wire driven in legal ways:
352	
353	```scala mdoc:reset
354	import chisel3._
355	import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig}
356	import chisel3.probe.{Probe, ProbeValue, define}
357	
358	object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
359	object B extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
360	
361	class Foo extends RawModule {
362	  val a = IO(Output(Probe(Bool(), A)))
363	  val b = IO(Output(Probe(Bool(), B)))
364	
365	  layer.block(A) {
366	    val a_wire = WireInit(false.B)
367	    define(a, ProbeValue(a_wire))
368	  }
369	
370	  val b_wire_probe = Wire(Probe(Bool(), B))
371	  define(b, b_wire_probe)
372	
373	  layer.block(B) {
374	    val b_wire = WireInit(false.B)
375	    define(b_wire_probe, ProbeValue(b_wire))
376	  }
377	
378	}
379	```
380	
381	Additionally, as the pattern of driving a layer-colored probe wire from within a
382	layer block is common, layer blocks are also capable of directly returning a
383	layer-colored wire.  To do this, the return value of a layer block must be a
384	subtype of `Data`.
385	
386	Using this feature, the second layer block can be rewritten as follows:
387	
388	``` scala mdoc:silent
389	class Bar extends RawModule {
390	  val b = IO(Output(Probe(Bool(), B)))
391	
392	  val b_wire_probe = layer.block(B) {
393	    val b_wire = WireInit(false.B)
394	    define(b_wire_probe, ProbeValue(b_wire))
395	  }
396	
397	  define(b, b_wire_probe)
398	}
399	```
400	
401	:::info
402	
403	In implementation, a returned value from a layer block will cause a wire to be
404	created before the layer block.  I.e., what is shown in module `Bar` is just a
405	Chisel shorthand for what is written in `Foo`.  Layer blocks, as described in
406	the FIRRTL specification, do not have the ability to return values.
407	
408	:::
409	
410	### Enabling Layers
411	
412	When working with layer-colored probes, it is often convenient to grant access
413	to probes of one or more colors.  E.g., testbenches often want to _enable_ all
414	layers in a design-under-test so that they gain access to layer-colored probe
415	ports necessary for advanced design verification.  Without an additional
416	feature, this use case is poorly supported with just layer coloring.  First, it
417	is tedious to enclose all code inside a testbench in a layer block.  Second, a
418	testbench may need to read probes with colors that do not have a parent--child
419	relationship.  No layer block is capable of both legally reading from different
420	probes and combining the results.
421	
422	To support this use case, Chisel provides the `layer.enable` API.  This API
423	grants access to any layer-colored probes of instantiated modules for the
424	enabled layer.  The API may be used more than once to enable more than one
425	layer.
426	
427	The example below instantiates module `Foo` from the previous section.  After
428	enabling layers `A` and `B`, the module can read from probes with colors `A` and
429	`B` and use their results in a single operation:
430	
431	```scala mdoc:silent
432	import chisel3.layer.enable
433	import chisel3.probe.read
434	
435	class Bar extends RawModule {
436	
437	  enable(A)
438	  enable(B)
439	
440	  val foo = Module(new Foo)
441	
442	  val c = read(foo.a) ^ read(foo.b)
443	
444	}
445	```
446	
447	## Examples
448	
449	### Simple Extract Layer
450	
451	The design below has a single extract layer that, when enabled, will add an
452	assert that checks for overflow.  Based on the FIRRTL ABI, we can expect that a
453	file called `layers-Foo-A.sv` will be produced when we compile it.
454	
455	```scala mdoc:reset:silent
456	import chisel3._
457	import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig, block}
458	import chisel3.ltl.AssertProperty
459	
460	object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
461	
462	class Foo extends Module {
463	  val a, b = IO(Input(UInt(4.W)))
464	  val sum = IO(Output(UInt(4.W)))
465	
466	  sum :<= a +% b
467	
468	  block(A) {
469	    withDisable(Disable.Never) {
470	      AssertProperty(!(a +& b)(4), "overflow occurred")
471	    }
472	  }
473	
474	}
475	```
476	
477	After compilation, we get the following SystemVerilog.  Comments that include
478	`FILE` indicate the beginning of a new file:
479	
480	```scala mdoc:verilog
481	circt.stage.ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilog(
482	  new Foo,
483	  firtoolOpts = Array(
484	    "-strip-debug-info",
485	    "-disable-all-randomization",
486	    "-enable-layers=Verification",
487	    "-enable-layers=Verification.Assert",
488	    "-enable-layers=Verification.Assume",
489	    "-enable-layers=Verification.Cover"
490	  )
491	)
492	```
493	
494	:::info
495	
496	The above example was compiled with the firtool options
497	`-enable-layers=Verification`, `-enable-layers=Verification.Assert`,
498	`-enable-layers=Verification.Assume`, and `-enable-layers=Verification.Cover` to
499	make the output terser.  Normally, bind files would show up for these built-in
500	layers.
501	
502	:::
503	
504	:::info
505	
506	Note: the generated module, `Foo_A`, and its file, `Foo_A.sv`, are _not part of
507	the ABI_.  You should not rely on any generated module names or files other than
508	the bind file, `layers-Foo-A.sv`.
509	
510	:::
511	
512	### Simple Inline Layer
513	
514	The design below is the same as the previous example, but uses an inline layer.
515	Based on the FIRRTL ABI, we can expect that the body of the layer block will be
516	guarded by an `` `ifdef `` sensitive to the preprocessor macro `layer$A`.
517	
518	```scala mdoc:reset:silent
519	import chisel3._
520	import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig, block}
521	import chisel3.ltl.AssertProperty
522	
523	object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
524	
525	class Foo extends Module {
526	  val a, b = IO(Input(UInt(4.W)))
527	  val sum = IO(Output(UInt(4.W)))
528	
529	  sum :<= a +% b
530	
531	  block(A) {
532	    withDisable(Disable.Never) {
533	      AssertProperty(!(a +& b)(4), "overflow occurred")
534	    }
535	  }
536	
537	}
538	```
539	
540	After compilation, we get the following SystemVerilog.
541	
542	```scala mdoc:verilog
543	circt.stage.ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilog(
544	  new Foo,
545	  firtoolOpts = Array(
546	    "-strip-debug-info",
547	    "-disable-all-randomization",
548	    "-enable-layers=Verification,Verification.Assert,Verification.Assume,Verification.Cover"
549	  )
550	)
551	```
552	
553	### Design Verification Example
554	
555	Consider a use case where a design or design verification engineer would like to
556	add some asserts and prints to a module.  The prints are for the creation of an
557	execution trace.  The logic necessary for the asserts and prints requires
558	additional computation.  All of this code should selectively included at Verilog
559	elaboration time (not at Chisel elaboration time).  The engineer can use three
560	layers to do this.
561	
562	There are three layers used in this example:
563	
564	1. The built-in `Verification` layer
565	1. The built-in `Assert` layer which is nested under the built-in `Verification`
566	   layer
567	1. A user-defined `Trace` layer which is also nested under the built-in
568	   `Verification` layer
569	
570	The `Verification` layer can be used to store common logic used by both the
571	`Assert` and `Trace` layers.  The latter two layers allow for separation of,
572	respectively, assertions from prints.
573	
574	One way to write this in Scala is the following:
575	
576	```scala mdoc:reset:silent
577	import chisel3._
578	import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig, block}
579	import chisel3.layers.Verification
580	
581	// User-defined layers are declared here.  Built-in layers do not need to be declared.
582	object UserDefined {
583	  implicit val root: Layer = Verification
584	  object Trace extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
585	}
586	
587	class Foo extends Module {
588	  val a = IO(Input(UInt(32.W)))
589	  val b = IO(Output(UInt(32.W)))
590	
591	  b := a +% 1.U
592	
593	  // This adds a `Verification` layer block inside Foo.
594	  block(Verification) {
595	
596	    // Some common logic added here.  The input port `a` is "captured" and
597	    // used here.
598	    val a_d0 = RegNext(a)
599	
600	    // This adds an `Assert` layer block.
601	    block(Verification.Assert) {
602	      chisel3.assert(a >= a_d0, "a must always increment")
603	    }
604	
605	    // This adds a `Debug` layer block.
606	    block(UserDefined.Trace) {
607	      printf("a: %x, a_d0: %x", a, a_d0)
608	    }
609	
610	  }
611	
612	}
613	
614	```
615	
616	After compilation, this will produce two layer include files with the
617	following filenames.  One file is created for each extract layer:
618	
619	1. `layers_Foo_Verification.sv`
620	1. `layers_Foo_Verification_Assert.sv`
621	
622	Additionally, the resulting SystemVerilog will be sensitive to the preprocessor
623	define `layer$Verification$Trace` due to the one inline layer we added.
624	
625	A user can then include any combination of these files in their design to
626	include the optional functionality described by the `Verification` or
627	`Verification.Assert` layers and enable debugging by setting the preprocessor
628	macro.  The `Verification.Assert` bind file automatically includes the
629	`Verification` bind file for the user.
630	
631	:::note
632	
633	By default, a Chisel `printf` will be put in the `Verification.Debug` extract
634	layer.  This default behavior was changed in the above example by putting the
635	`printf` inside the user-defined `Trace` inline layer.
636	
637	:::
638	
639	#### Implementation Notes
640	
641	:::warning
642	
643	This section describes the implementation of how layers are compiled.  Anything
644	that is _not_ a bind file name or a preprocessor macro should not be relied
645	upon!  A FIRRTL compiler may implement this differently or may optimize layer
646	blocks in any legal way it chooses.  E.g., layer blocks associated with the same
647	layer may be merged, layer blocks may be moved up or down the hierarchy, code
648	that only fans out to a layer block may be sunk into it, and unused layer blocks
649	may be deleted.
650	
651	The information below is for user understanding and interest only.
652	
653	:::
654	
655	In implementation, a FIRRTL compiler creates three Verilog modules for the
656	circuit above (one for `Foo` and one for each layer block associated with an
657	extract layer in module `Foo`):
658	
659	1. `Foo`
660	1. `Foo_Verification`
661	1. `Foo_Verification_Assert`
662	
663	These will typically be created in separate files with names that match the
664	modules, i.e., `Foo.sv`, `Foo_Verification.sv`, and
665	`Foo_Verification_Assert.sv`.
666	
667	The ports of each module created from a layer block will be automatically
668	determined based on what that layer block captured from outside the layer block.
669	In the example above, the `Verification` layer block captured port `a`.  The
670	`Assert` layer block captured captured `a` and `a_d0`.
671	
672	:::info
673	
674	Even though there are no layer blocks that use the `Verification.Assume` or
675	`Verification.Cover` layers, bind files which have no effect are produced in the
676	output.  This is due to the ABI which requires that layers that are defined in
677	FIRRTL must produce these files.
678	
679	:::
680	
681	#### Verilog Output
682	
683	The complete Verilog output for this example is reproduced below:
684	
685	```scala mdoc:verilog
686	// Use ChiselStage instead of chisel3.docs.emitSystemVerilog because we want layers printed here (obviously)
687	import circt.stage.ChiselStage
688	ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilog(new Foo, firtoolOpts=Array("-strip-debug-info", "-disable-all-randomization"))
689	```
690

file.content:
---
layout: docs
title:  "Layers"
section: "chisel3"
---

# Layers

Layers describe functionality of a Chisel generator that a user would like to
_optionally_ include at Verilog elaboration time.  Pragmatically, they are a
feature to access SystemVerilog's `bind` construct and `` `ifdef `` preprocessor
macros.  The optional functionality of layers, by construction, is not allowed
to affect logic outside the layer.

Layers are typically used to describe design verification code or
debugging logic that a user would like to be able to later disable (for
performance, verbosity, or cleanliness reasons) or use internally, but exclude
from delivery to a customer.

## Overview

A layer consists of two pieces:

1. A layer _declaration_, and
1. One or more _layer blocks_ inside Chisel modules.

The declaration indicates that optional functionality can exist.  The layer
block contains the optional functionality.

There are two kinds of layers.  The layer kind determines the _convention_,
i.e., how the layer blocks of a layer are represented in Verilog and the
mechanism to enable a layer.  Available layer kinds are:

1. "Extract" Layers: layers whose blocks are lowered to modules that are
   instantiated using `bind` and can be enabled by including a file during
   Verilog elaboration, and
2. "Inline" Layers: layers whose blocks will be guarded with `` `ifdef `` macros
   and can be enabled by setting a Verilog preprocessor define.

Extract layers may also specify a directory into which their collateral are
written.

:::info

For more information about these SystemVerilog concepts, the IEEE 1800-2023
standard discusses `bind` in Section 23.11 and `` `ifdef `` in Section 23.6.

:::

To declare a layer, create a singleton `object` in scala that extends the
abstract class `chisel3.layer.Layer`, passing into the layer constructor either
an object of class `chisel3.layer.LayerConfig.Extract` for an extract layer, or
the object `chisel3.layer.LayerConfig.Inline` for an inline layer.

Below, an extract layer and an inline layer are declared:

```scala mdoc:silent
import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig}

object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())

object B extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
```

Layers may be nested.  Nesting a child layer under a parent layer means that the
child layer may access constructs in the parent layer.  Put differently, the
child layer will only be enabled if the parent layer is already enabled.  To
declare a nested layer, extend the `chisel3.layer.Layer` abstract class inside
another declaration.

The following example defines an extract layer with two nested layers inside it:

```scala mdoc:silent
object C extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract()) {
  object D extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
  object E extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline) {
    object F extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
  }
}
```

:::info

SystemVerilog prohibits a `bind` instantiation under another `bind`
instantiation.  However, Chisel allows nesting of extract layers.  This is
resolved by the FIRRTL compiler to restructure nested extract layers to be
sibling modules that communicate via ports.

:::

:::warning

Extract layers may not be nested under inline layers.  However, inline layers
may be nested under extract layers.

Any module which contains layer blocks or transitively contains layer blocks in
its submodules may not be instantiated under a layer block.

:::

A _layer block_, associated with a layer, adds optional functionality to a
module that is enabled if that layer is enabled.  To define a layer block, use
the `chisel3.layer.block` inside a Chisel module and pass the layer that it
should be associated with.

Inside the layer block, any Chisel or Scala value visible in lexical scope may
be used.  Layer blocks may not return values.  Any values created inside a layer
block are not accessible outside the layer block, unless using layer-colored
probes.

The following example defines layer blocks inside module `Foo`.  Each layer
block contains a wire that captures a value from its visible lexical scope.
(For nested layer blocks, this scope includes their parent layer blocks.):

```scala mdoc:silent
import chisel3._
import chisel3.layer.block

class Foo extends RawModule {
  val port = IO(Input(Bool()))

  block(A) {
    val a = WireInit(port)
  }

  block(B) {
    val b = WireInit(port)
  }

  block(C) {
    val c = WireInit(port)
    block(C.D) {
      val d = WireInit(port | c)
    }
    block(C.E) {
      val e = WireInit(port ^ c)
      block (C.E.F) {
        val f = WireInit(port & e)
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The layer block API will automatically create parent layer blocks for you if
possible.  In the following example, it is legal to directly create a layer
block of `C.D` directly in a module:

```scala mdoc:silent
class Bar extends RawModule {
  block (C.D) {}
}
```

Formally, it is legal to create a layer block associated with a layer as long as
the current scope is an _ancestor_ of the request layer.

:::info

The requirement is an _ancestor_ relationship, not a _proper ancestor_
relationship.  This means that it is legal to nest a layer block under a layer
block of the same layer like:

```scala mdoc:silent
class Baz extends RawModule {
  block(A) {
    block(A) {}
  }
}
```

:::

## Verilog ABI

Layers are compiled to SystemVerilog using the FIRRTL ABI.  This ABI defines
what happens to layer blocks in a Chisel design and how a layer can be enabled
after a design is compiled to SystemVerilog.

:::info

For the exact definition of the FIRRTL ABI for layers, see the [FIRRTL ABI
Specification](https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl-spec/releases/latest/download/abi.pdf).

:::

### Extract Layers

Extract layers have their layer blocks removed from the design.  To enable a
layer, a file with a specific name should be included in the design.  This file
begins with `layers-` and then includes the circuit name and all layer names
delimited with dashes (`-`).

For example, for module `Foo` declared above, this will produce three files, one
for each extract layer:

```
layers-Foo-A.sv
layers-Foo-C.sv
layers-Foo-C-D.sv
```

To enable any of these layers at compilation time, the appropriate file should
be included in the build.  Any combination of files may be included.  Including
only a child layer's file will automatically include its parent layer's file.

### Inline Layers

Inline layers have their layer blocks guarded with conditional compilation
directives.  To enable an inline layer, set a preprocessor define when compiling
your design.  The preprocessor define begins with `layer$` and then includes the
all layer names delimited with dollar signs (`$`).  Parent extract layer names
appear in the macro.

For example, for module `Foo` declared above, this will be sensitive to three
macros, one for each inline layer:

```
layer$B
layer$C$E
layer$C$E$F
```

## User-defined Layers

A user is free to define as many layers as they want.  All layers shown
previously are user-defined, e.g., `A` and `C.E` are user-defined layers.
User-defined layers are only emitted into FIRRTL if they have layer block users.
To change this behavior and unconditionally emit a user-defined layer, use the
`chisel3.layer.addLayer` API.

:::tip

Before creating new user-defined layers, consider using the built-in layers
defined below.  Additionally, if working in a larger project, the project may
have it's own user-defined layers that you are expected to use.  This is because
the ABIs affect the build system.  Please consult with a technical lead of the
project to see if this is the case.

:::

## Built-in Layers

Chisel provides several built-in layers.  These are shown below with their full
Scala paths.  All layers except the temporal layers are extract layers.
Temporal layers are inline:

```
chisel3.layers.Verification                          (Extract)
├── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assert               (Extract)
│   └── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assert.Temporal  (Inline)
├── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assume               (Extract)
│   └── chisel3.layers.Verification.Assume.Temporal  (Inline)
├── chisel3.layers.Verification.Cover                (Extract)
│   └── chisel3.layers.Verification.Cover.Temporal   (Inline)
└── chisel3.layers.Verification.Debug                (Extract)
```

These built-in layers are dual purpose.  First, these layers match the common
use case of sequestering verification code.  The `Verification` layer is for
common verification collateral.  The `Assert`, `Assume`, and `Cover` layers are
for, respectively, assertions, assumptions, and cover statements.  The
`Temporal` layers can be used to sequester _temporal_ properties as these are
not fully supported by simulation tools.  Second, the Chisel standard library
uses them for a number of its APIs.  _Unless otherwise wrapped in a different
layer block, the following operations are automatically placed in layers_:

* Assertions are placed in the `Verification.Assert` layer
* Assumptions are placed in the `Verification.Assume` layer
* Covers are placed in the `Verification.Cover` layer
* Prints are placed in the `Verification.Debug` layer

At this time, no operations are automatically placed in `Temporal` layers.  The
`Temporal` layer is up to the user to use as needed.

:::tip

User-defined layers may want to define their own `Temporal` layer.  To ease
this, Chisel provides the `chisel3.layers.HasTemporalInlineLayer` which, when
mixed-in to a user-defined layer, will add a child inline temporal layer.

:::

:::warning

Users of the ChiselSim library should manually disable `Temporal` layers when
using `Temporal` layers to describe properties that their chosen simulator
cannot suppot.  If using ChiselSim's `Cli.Simulator` trait to support multiple
backends, then the Verilator backend will automatically disable temporal layers.

:::

For predictability of output, these layers will always be show up in the FIRRTL
that Chisel emits.  To change this behavior, use `firtool` command line options
to _specialize_ these layers (remove their optionality by making them always
enabled or disabled).  Use `-enable-layers` to enable a layer, `-disable-layers`
to disable a layer, or `-default-layer-specialization` to set a default
specialization.

:::tip

Users may extend built-in layers with user-defined layers using an advanced API.
To do this, the layer parent must be specified as an implicit value.

The following example nests the layer `Debug` to the `Verification` layer:

```scala mdoc:silent
object UserDefined {
  // Define an implicit val `root` of type `Layer` to cause layers which can see
  // this to use `root` as their parent layer.  This allows us to nest the
  // user-defined `Debug` layer under the built-in `Verification` layer.
  implicit val root: Layer = chisel3.layers.Verification
  object Debug extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
}
```

:::

## Layer-coloring

While layers are not allowed to influence the design or their parent layers, it
is often useful and necessary to allow layer blocks to send information out of
their containing modules to be read by layer blocks of the same layer or
children layers. Hardware which has this optional property is said to be
_layer-colored_.  Both probes and wires can be layer-colored.

### Layer-colored Probes and Wires

A layer-colored probe is a probe that exists if a user enables its corresponding
layer during Verilog elaboration.  Layer-colored probes are used to describe
optional verification, debugging, or logging interfaces.

Layer-colored wires are used as temporary storage of defined probe values.  They
are used for communication between layer blocks of the same layer in the same
module or as temporary storage when forwarding a probe to a port.

A layer-colored probe or wire may be the target of a `define` if the `define` is
enabled when the color of the probe or wire is enabled.  A layer-colored probe
or wire may be `read` from if the color of the probe or wire is enabled when the
`read` is enabled.  Put differently, you may write to your layer or a child
layer and you may read from your layer or a parent layer.

:::info

For more information, see the layer coloring section of the [FIRRTL
Specification](https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl-spec/releases/latest/download/spec.pdf).

:::

The example below shows two layer-colored probe ports and one layer-colored
probe wire driven in legal ways:

```scala mdoc:reset
import chisel3._
import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig}
import chisel3.probe.{Probe, ProbeValue, define}

object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())
object B extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())

class Foo extends RawModule {
  val a = IO(Output(Probe(Bool(), A)))
  val b = IO(Output(Probe(Bool(), B)))

  layer.block(A) {
    val a_wire = WireInit(false.B)
    define(a, ProbeValue(a_wire))
  }

  val b_wire_probe = Wire(Probe(Bool(), B))
  define(b, b_wire_probe)

  layer.block(B) {
    val b_wire = WireInit(false.B)
    define(b_wire_probe, ProbeValue(b_wire))
  }

}
```

Additionally, as the pattern of driving a layer-colored probe wire from within a
layer block is common, layer blocks are also capable of directly returning a
layer-colored wire.  To do this, the return value of a layer block must be a
subtype of `Data`.

Using this feature, the second layer block can be rewritten as follows:

``` scala mdoc:silent
class Bar extends RawModule {
  val b = IO(Output(Probe(Bool(), B)))

  val b_wire_probe = layer.block(B) {
    val b_wire = WireInit(false.B)
    define(b_wire_probe, ProbeValue(b_wire))
  }

  define(b, b_wire_probe)
}
```

:::info

In implementation, a returned value from a layer block will cause a wire to be
created before the layer block.  I.e., what is shown in module `Bar` is just a
Chisel shorthand for what is written in `Foo`.  Layer blocks, as described in
the FIRRTL specification, do not have the ability to return values.

:::

### Enabling Layers

When working with layer-colored probes, it is often convenient to grant access
to probes of one or more colors.  E.g., testbenches often want to _enable_ all
layers in a design-under-test so that they gain access to layer-colored probe
ports necessary for advanced design verification.  Without an additional
feature, this use case is poorly supported with just layer coloring.  First, it
is tedious to enclose all code inside a testbench in a layer block.  Second, a
testbench may need to read probes with colors that do not have a parent--child
relationship.  No layer block is capable of both legally reading from different
probes and combining the results.

To support this use case, Chisel provides the `layer.enable` API.  This API
grants access to any layer-colored probes of instantiated modules for the
enabled layer.  The API may be used more than once to enable more than one
layer.

The example below instantiates module `Foo` from the previous section.  After
enabling layers `A` and `B`, the module can read from probes with colors `A` and
`B` and use their results in a single operation:

```scala mdoc:silent
import chisel3.layer.enable
import chisel3.probe.read

class Bar extends RawModule {

  enable(A)
  enable(B)

  val foo = Module(new Foo)

  val c = read(foo.a) ^ read(foo.b)

}
```

## Examples

### Simple Extract Layer

The design below has a single extract layer that, when enabled, will add an
assert that checks for overflow.  Based on the FIRRTL ABI, we can expect that a
file called `layers-Foo-A.sv` will be produced when we compile it.

```scala mdoc:reset:silent
import chisel3._
import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig, block}
import chisel3.ltl.AssertProperty

object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Extract())

class Foo extends Module {
  val a, b = IO(Input(UInt(4.W)))
  val sum = IO(Output(UInt(4.W)))

  sum :<= a +% b

  block(A) {
    withDisable(Disable.Never) {
      AssertProperty(!(a +& b)(4), "overflow occurred")
    }
  }

}
```

After compilation, we get the following SystemVerilog.  Comments that include
`FILE` indicate the beginning of a new file:

```scala mdoc:verilog
circt.stage.ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilog(
  new Foo,
  firtoolOpts = Array(
    "-strip-debug-info",
    "-disable-all-randomization",
    "-enable-layers=Verification",
    "-enable-layers=Verification.Assert",
    "-enable-layers=Verification.Assume",
    "-enable-layers=Verification.Cover"
  )
)
```

:::info

The above example was compiled with the firtool options
`-enable-layers=Verification`, `-enable-layers=Verification.Assert`,
`-enable-layers=Verification.Assume`, and `-enable-layers=Verification.Cover` to
make the output terser.  Normally, bind files would show up for these built-in
layers.

:::

:::info

Note: the generated module, `Foo_A`, and its file, `Foo_A.sv`, are _not part of
the ABI_.  You should not rely on any generated module names or files other than
the bind file, `layers-Foo-A.sv`.

:::

### Simple Inline Layer

The design below is the same as the previous example, but uses an inline layer.
Based on the FIRRTL ABI, we can expect that the body of the layer block will be
guarded by an `` `ifdef `` sensitive to the preprocessor macro `layer$A`.

```scala mdoc:reset:silent
import chisel3._
import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig, block}
import chisel3.ltl.AssertProperty

object A extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)

class Foo extends Module {
  val a, b = IO(Input(UInt(4.W)))
  val sum = IO(Output(UInt(4.W)))

  sum :<= a +% b

  block(A) {
    withDisable(Disable.Never) {
      AssertProperty(!(a +& b)(4), "overflow occurred")
    }
  }

}
```

After compilation, we get the following SystemVerilog.

```scala mdoc:verilog
circt.stage.ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilog(
  new Foo,
  firtoolOpts = Array(
    "-strip-debug-info",
    "-disable-all-randomization",
    "-enable-layers=Verification,Verification.Assert,Verification.Assume,Verification.Cover"
  )
)
```

### Design Verification Example

Consider a use case where a design or design verification engineer would like to
add some asserts and prints to a module.  The prints are for the creation of an
execution trace.  The logic necessary for the asserts and prints requires
additional computation.  All of this code should selectively included at Verilog
elaboration time (not at Chisel elaboration time).  The engineer can use three
layers to do this.

There are three layers used in this example:

1. The built-in `Verification` layer
1. The built-in `Assert` layer which is nested under the built-in `Verification`
   layer
1. A user-defined `Trace` layer which is also nested under the built-in
   `Verification` layer

The `Verification` layer can be used to store common logic used by both the
`Assert` and `Trace` layers.  The latter two layers allow for separation of,
respectively, assertions from prints.

One way to write this in Scala is the following:

```scala mdoc:reset:silent
import chisel3._
import chisel3.layer.{Layer, LayerConfig, block}
import chisel3.layers.Verification

// User-defined layers are declared here.  Built-in layers do not need to be declared.
object UserDefined {
  implicit val root: Layer = Verification
  object Trace extends Layer(LayerConfig.Inline)
}

class Foo extends Module {
  val a = IO(Input(UInt(32.W)))
  val b = IO(Output(UInt(32.W)))

  b := a +% 1.U

  // This adds a `Verification` layer block inside Foo.
  block(Verification) {

    // Some common logic added here.  The input port `a` is "captured" and
    // used here.
    val a_d0 = RegNext(a)

    // This adds an `Assert` layer block.
    block(Verification.Assert) {
      chisel3.assert(a >= a_d0, "a must always increment")
    }

    // This adds a `Debug` layer block.
    block(UserDefined.Trace) {
      printf("a: %x, a_d0: %x", a, a_d0)
    }

  }

}

```

After compilation, this will produce two layer include files with the
following filenames.  One file is created for each extract layer:

1. `layers_Foo_Verification.sv`
1. `layers_Foo_Verification_Assert.sv`

Additionally, the resulting SystemVerilog will be sensitive to the preprocessor
define `layer$Verification$Trace` due to the one inline layer we added.

A user can then include any combination of these files in their design to
include the optional functionality described by the `Verification` or
`Verification.Assert` layers and enable debugging by setting the preprocessor
macro.  The `Verification.Assert` bind file automatically includes the
`Verification` bind file for the user.

:::note

By default, a Chisel `printf` will be put in the `Verification.Debug` extract
layer.  This default behavior was changed in the above example by putting the
`printf` inside the user-defined `Trace` inline layer.

:::

#### Implementation Notes

:::warning

This section describes the implementation of how layers are compiled.  Anything
that is _not_ a bind file name or a preprocessor macro should not be relied
upon!  A FIRRTL compiler may implement this differently or may optimize layer
blocks in any legal way it chooses.  E.g., layer blocks associated with the same
layer may be merged, layer blocks may be moved up or down the hierarchy, code
that only fans out to a layer block may be sunk into it, and unused layer blocks
may be deleted.

The information below is for user understanding and interest only.

:::

In implementation, a FIRRTL compiler creates three Verilog modules for the
circuit above (one for `Foo` and one for each layer block associated with an
extract layer in module `Foo`):

1. `Foo`
1. `Foo_Verification`
1. `Foo_Verification_Assert`

These will typically be created in separate files with names that match the
modules, i.e., `Foo.sv`, `Foo_Verification.sv`, and
`Foo_Verification_Assert.sv`.

The ports of each module created from a layer block will be automatically
determined based on what that layer block captured from outside the layer block.
In the example above, the `Verification` layer block captured port `a`.  The
`Assert` layer block captured captured `a` and `a_d0`.

:::info

Even though there are no layer blocks that use the `Verification.Assume` or
`Verification.Cover` layers, bind files which have no effect are produced in the
output.  This is due to the ABI which requires that layers that are defined in
FIRRTL must produce these files.

:::

#### Verilog Output

The complete Verilog output for this example is reproduced below:

```scala mdoc:verilog
// Use ChiselStage instead of chisel3.docs.emitSystemVerilog because we want layers printed here (obviously)
import circt.stage.ChiselStage
ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilog(new Foo, firtoolOpts=Array("-strip-debug-info", "-disable-all-randomization"))
```
